figures.caption: Table 1: Baseline families and execution interfaces under constrained views.

Figure 1: Object-centric view planning in a tabletop robot deployment. Starting from a home pose, the robot has to select feasible camera poses to reconstruct an object with unknown geometry. Hereby, different object geometries and placements induce different reachable-view subsets: green views are executable, while black views are filtered out by inverse kinematics and collision checks. The transparent robot overlays illustrate representative feasible actions under different trials.

Table 3: Path-efficient top-3 methods under different budget regimes in the whole view space. Methods are first filtered by NSC@0.02 ≥ 0.95 and then ranked by path cost. In fixed-budget settings, lower path cost should be interpreted together with Views because some iterative methods may terminate early under their native action interface.
Table 2: Quality-oriented top-3 methods under different budget regimes in the whole view space. Methods are ranked by NSC@0.02. Views include the standardized initial observation.

Table 4: Reachability-induced degradation under each method’s maximum-view deployment setting. For each method, we compare whole (W) and quarter (Q) view spaces under the evaluated budget protocol that executes the most views. ∆NSC and ∆SC are computed as W minus Q.

Figure 2: Difficulty characterization of ObjView-Bench. (a) Representative objects are ordered by observation saturation difficulty dsat and overlaid on the marginal gain curve; the arrow marks the 128-view protocol. (b) Planning difficulty dplan is long-tailed in the main pool. (c) Observation saturation difficulty dsat and planning difficulty dplan are related but not equivalent.

Figure 3: Planning difficulty-aware sampling. (a) Balanced sampling increases exposure to medium- and high-dplan objects compared with raw sampling. (b) This improves MA-SCVP achieved voxel coverage across the planning-difficulty axis.
